content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 30, 2017

Jim Lombard
5613 Darby Road
Rocklin, CA 95765

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-16-234

Dear Mr. Lombard:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act, we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Because your question is general in nature and not limited to specific governmental proceedings, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

If you left your current state employment with Covered California would your past involvement in the State's 21st Century and FISCal Projects preclude you from providing consulting services to a (1) contractor on a state payroll system or (2) vendor under FISCal?

CONCLUSION

The Act prohibits you from consulting or advising a contractor on a state payroll system or vendor under FISCal only if the consulting work is determined to be the same proceeding you participated in as a former State official. However, you are not prohibited from providing consulting services to a (1) contractor on a new state payroll system or (2) vendor under FISCal if you were not previously involved in the vendor's contract.³

___________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

³ We note that it is unnecessary to further consider Section 1090 because any restrictions imposed under on the activity you have described are fully encompassed within the prohibitions of the Act's permanent ban.

File No. I-16-234
Page No. 2

FACTS

You have been employed with Covered California since July 2014. Prior to this, you were
the Chief Administrative Officer with the State Controller's Office (SCO) for about seven years.
During this time, you were also the executive sponsor for the 21st Century Project (a large
information-technology project replacing the State's legacy payroll system) and a steering
committee member on the FISCal project (a large information-technology project replacing the
State's legacy procurement and accounting system).

The 21st Century Project was largely run by the SCO with steering committee
representatives from other agencies. The State terminated the system integrator and was in litigation
regarding the 21st Century Project that was resolved last year. You have not been involved in the
21st Century Project in the last two years. You ask if your involvement with the 21st Century
Project precludes you from working with a contractor that may provide services to the State on a
state payroll project.

With regard to FISCal, the project is being rolled out by agencies in waves and Covered
California is contemplating rolling out FISCal soon. Considering your prior work as a steering
committee member, you ask if there are any limitations on working with a FISCal state vendor that
is not working directly with Covered California. You understand that the one-year ban potentially
applies to any appearance or communication before Covered California and, therefore, do not
intend to make any appearance or communication before Covered California.

The consulting services that you are contemplating include project management, testing,
strategic planning, business continuity planning, independent validation and verification (IV and
V), etc. You ask whether any of these activities would be precluded for the state payroll project or
FISCal.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. In addition,
Sections 87407 and 87100 prohibit officials from making, participating in making, or using their
position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or
have any arrangement concerning employment. These provisions are commonly referred to as the
"revolving door" prohibitions. Because you have stated that you do not intend to appear before or
communicate with Covered California within 12 months of leaving state service, we address only
the permanent ban.

The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating, for compensation, in certain proceedings involving the State of California and other
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state
employee participated while employed by the State. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of
absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(1).)

File No. I-16-234
Page No. 3

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any
oral or written communication – or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or
communication – made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. "'Judicial, quasi-
judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency. . . .'"
(Section 87400(c).)

Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of
confidential information . . . .'" (Section 87400(d).)

However, "[t]he permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where
the new proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had
participated. A 'new' proceeding on the surface typically involves different parties, a different
subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; see also Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not participate
are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new contract is one that is
based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. I-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.)

21st Century Project

You have indicated that the 21st Century Project was delayed by the termination of the
system integrator and subsequent litigation. While you ask whether your involvement with the 21st
Century Project prohibits you from working as a consultant for a contractor that may provide
services to the State on a state payroll project, you have not identified the actual governmental
proceeding involving a state payroll project. On the facts before us, we can generally state that the
Act does not preclude you from working as a consultant for a state contractor. However, in
consulting to the contractor, you may not consult or provide advice regarding proceedings you
previously participated in.

Unfortunately, we are unable to determine if a project is the same proceeding as the 21st
Century Project without a thorough description of the specific state payroll project you may wish to
consult on. We can, however, provide some general guidance to assist you in making this
determination. If the 21st Century Project has terminated and a subsequent payroll project begins
anew, your past involvement in the 21st Century Project would not preclude from providing
consulting services to a contractor on the new payroll project. However, if the subsequent payroll
project merely continues upon the previous 21st Century Project, you may be prohibited under the
Act from consulting to a contractor regarding the project. For example, you would not be able to
consult to a contractor on a contract to act as a new system integrator to continue where the
terminated system integrator left off.

File No. I-16-234
Page No. 4


FISCal

In regard to consulting or advising a vendor under the FISCal project, you are again
prohibited by the Act from consulting or advising a vendor regarding the services they are
providing the State if you previously participated in the State's contract with the vendor. However,
as the roll out of FISCal has already begun, after you leave state service you are not precluded from
consulting or advising a vendor regarding the vendor's contract with the State or the general FISCal
requirements if you were not involved in the vendor's contract with the State as a former State
official.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. In addition, 
Sections 87407 and 87100 prohibit officials from making, participating in making, or using their 
position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, or

specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in Which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the State. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation

absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741. l(a)(l ).)

Fl$Cal

prohibited by the Act from consulting or advising a vendor regarding the services they are
